In this module, we're going to cover your financing options and ultimately attracting the financing you'll need to fund your business acquisitions. To kick us off, I want to cover the different financing options you can tap into. Remember, we're looking at acquiring a business without using any of your own money. So it's important that you understand that there are a lot of options out there to help you do just that. In future lessons, I'll cover all of these financing options more in depth, what you'll need and the different components involved in the process. So let's get started. I'm going to cover how you can leverage surplus cash, inventory, fixed assets like equipment, real estate, all kinds of ways to help finance the acquisition of the business. We'll look at loans from Small Business Administration or SBA loans that you might qualify for or help fund your deal. I'll cover the process of financing intellectual property and the possibility of equity financing from customers, suppliers, or partners in the business. We'll take a look at how you can even borrow money based on the strength of the business's cash flow through something called cash flow-based financing. We're going to go more in depth on seller financing and all the terminology you'll need to know. I'll go over earn-outs. This isn't a guaranteed payment. It's based on performance, matrix of the business that happens after you close. But I want to cover it because it can be used as a great financing option. We're going to tackle equity crowdfunding, which is a relatively new financing option. I'm going to talk about the different types of investors who can help finance the deal as well. People like angel investors, private equity, venture capitalists, high net worth people, friends, or family, all the different types of investors that can fund your deal. We'll also talk about grant funding. You can use government grant funding to acquire a business, but there are ways to leverage this kind of funding immediately after the deal is done.
